text: Prospective cohort study of ultra-processed food intake and depressive symptoms in 14,200 adults followed for five years. Dietary questionnaires were used at baseline. Higher ultra-processed food intake was associated with a greater likelihood of later depressive symptoms after statistical adjustment. No intervention was tested.

draft.bottomLine: This paper reports a meaningful association between higher ultra-processed food intake and later depressive symptoms. It is useful evidence for a pattern, but not a clean causal answer.

whatPaperActuallyShows.observedResult: Higher ultra-processed food intake was associated with greater likelihood of later depressive symptoms

keyFindings.finding: Higher ultra-processed food intake tracked with more later depressive symptoms.
keyFindings.effectDirection: Higher intake linked to worse later mood outcomes
keyFindings.plainEnglishMeaning: Diet quality may matter for mental health, but this study cannot isolate cause and effect.

trustRating.reason: Large prospective cohorts are valuable for spotting patterns over time, but they still cannot rule out residual confounding or reverse causality.
trustRating.citationUse: Best cited as observational support for a pattern, not as decisive proof.

realWorldMeaning.summary: A smart reader should treat this as part of a broader nutrition-and-mental-health story rather than a standalone proof that one category of food directly drives depression.
realWorldMeaning.sensibleInterpretation: A reasonable takeaway is that diet quality is worth paying attention to, while keeping in mind that many social, behavioral, and health factors move together.
